step.title: Step 18
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='Move UP to align with Blue')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 86, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: After grabbing Blue and moving DOWN, only Green moved (25-28 → 31-34), Red stayed at 13-16. Blue still grabbed at cols 24-27. Retract didn't release Blue. Need to release Blue first, then try different strategy.
- Goal model: Navigate the agent to connect to colored blocks in the order shown in bottom panel: Red → Green → Blue.
- Action model: UP/DOWN moves agent vertically by 6 rows. RIGHT extends the path rightward. Need to test what happens when path reaches a colored block.
- Plan: Release Blue by retracting more LEFT, then grab Green and reposition.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 85 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 1.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Blue is still at cols 24-27 after more LEFT actions. The retract is not releasing Blue. Let me think about why.
Looking at the earlier successful retract:
- After extending RIGHT: Green at cols 42-45
- After retracting LEFT x10: Green at cols 18-21 (released)
But now:
- After extending RIGHT: Blue at cols 24-27
- After retracting LEFT x10: Blue still at cols 24-27
The difference is that earlier, Green was at cols 42-45 (far right), and retracting brought it back to 18-21. But now, Blue is at cols 24-27 (only 6 cols right of 18-21), and retracting didn't bring it back.
Wait, maybe the issue is that the agent moved DOWN after grabbing Blue. When the agent moves, the held block stays in place. But the path might have been broken or changed. Let me check if the path is still connected to Blue.
Actually, I think the issue is that after moving DOWN, the agent is no longer aligned with Blue. The agent is at rows 24-29, and Blue is at rows 19-22. They don't overlap. So the path might have been broken, and Blue is now "stuck" at cols 24-27.
Let me try moving UP to align with Blue again, then retract.
Current state:
- Agent: 24-29
- Red: 13-16, cols 18-21
- Blue: 19-22, cols 24-27 (grabbed but misaligned)
- Green: 31-34, cols 18-21
If I move UP to 18-23, the agent overlaps with Blue at 19-22. Then I can try retracting.
But wait, maybe the retract works differently. Let me check the segmentation to see if there's a path node connecting the agent to Blue.
Actually, let me try a different approach. Instead of trying to release Blue, let me just work with the current state. The blocks are:
- Red: 13-16, cols 18-21
- Blue: 19-22, cols 24-27
- Green: 31-34, cols 18-21
The target order is Red, Green, Blue. Currently it's Red, Blue, Green (by row). I need to swap Blue and Green.
One idea: Grab Green, move agent UP past Blue, then retract. This would place Green above Blue.
But first, I need to align with Green. The agent is at 24-29, and Green is at 31-34. They don't overlap. So I need to move DOWN to 30-35 to overlap with Green at 31-34.
But wait, Blue is still grabbed (cols 24-27). If I move DOWN, what happens to Blue? Let me check.
Actually, I think the issue is that Blue is "stuck" because the path was broken when the agent moved DOWN. Let me try moving UP to align with Blue, then see if I can release it.
Let me try: UP, then LEFT, and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Blue still grabbed at cols 24-27 after retracting. Agent at 24-29, Blue at 19-22 - not aligned. Path may be broken. Need to realign with Blue to release it.
Plan: Move UP to align with Blue, then try retracting to release.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: Agent 24-29, Blue 19-22 (cols 24-27, grabbed but misaligned)
# Move UP to align with Blue, then try retract

predict(expect_change=True, note='Move UP to align with Blue')
action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("=== After UP ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] == 16:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  Agent: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}")

# Now try retracting
predict(expect_change=True, note='Retract to release Blue')
action(['LEFT'] * 3)

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("\n=== After retract ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] == 16:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 24-27
  N: rows 31-34, cols 18-21
  Agent: rows 18-23

=== After retract ===
  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 24-27
  N: rows 31-34, cols 18-21
